<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Togakushi Shrine]][[de:Togakushi-Schrein]][[en:Togakushi Shrine]][[fr:Togakushi-jinja]][[it:Santuario Togakushi]][[ja:戸隠神社]][[zh:戶隱神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q218828]] Wikidata name: Togakushi Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:58, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nagano Prefecture, Japan}} {{more citations needed|date=June 2022}} {{cleanup rewrite|date=May 2013}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{Nihongo|'''Togakushi Shrine'''|戸隠神社|Togakushi Jinja}} | image = File:160430 Togakushi-jinja Chusha Nagano Japan02n.jpg | caption = The Lower Shrine, Hōkō-sha | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|36.742504|N|138.085128|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity ={{ill|Ame-no-tajikarao|qid=Q849412}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[:en:Togakushi, Nagano|Togakushi]], [[:en:Nagano City|Nagano City]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | prefecture = {{ill|Nagano|qid=Q128849}} | leadership = }} [[File:160430 Togakushi-jinja Chusha Nagano Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|The middle shrine Chū-sha]] [[File:Togakusi okusya.jpg|thumb|The upper shrine Oku-sha]] [[File:Tree lined path to the Togakushi shrine1.jpg|thumb|220x220px|Planted in the 17th century, the cedar-lined approach to the upper shrine Oku-sha]] The {{Nihongo|'''Togakushi Shrine'''|戸隠神社|Togakushi Jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Togakushi, Nagano|Togakushi]], [[:en:Nagano (city)|Nagano City]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The shrine is at the base of [[:en:Mount Togakushi|Mount Togakushi]] ({{Convert|1904|m|ft}}) in [[Myōkō-Togakushi Renzan National Park]].<ref>{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru Daijisen | title = Togakushiyama | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-12-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | oclc = 56431036 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }}</ref>
== Shrines == Togakushi Shrine consists of five shrines that can be visited in any order:
* The Lower Shrine (宝光社, ''Hōkō-sha'') enshrines the deity Ame-no-Uwaharu-no-Mikoto. It is believed to offer blessings related to academic life, [[:en:sewing|sewing]], safe [[:en:Childbirth|delivery]], and the protection of women and children.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=戸隠神社 {{!}} 霊山・戸隠山の麓を中心に創建された二千年余りに及ぶ歴史を刻む神社 |url=https://www.togakushi-jinja.jp/english/ |access-date=2024-08-20 |website=www.togakushi-jinja.jp |language=ja}}</ref> * ''Hinomiko-sha'' (火之御子社) enshrines four deities with the main deity being [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]]. There are two 500 year old [[:en:Cryptomeria|Japanese cedars]] known as the "husband and wife cedars" (夫婦の杉, ''Fufu-no-Sugi)'' and a famous [[:en:Cherry blossom|Cherry blossom]] tree known as [[:en:Saigyō|Saigyo]] Zakura. The shrine is believed to offer blessings for marriage, love, fire prevention, and entertainment.<ref name=":0" /> * The Middle Shrine (中社, ''Chū-sha'') has the head office of Togakushi Shrine. It enshrines [[Omoikane|Ame-no-Yagokoro-Omoikane-no-Mikoto]] and has several Japanese cedar trees ranging from 700 to 800 years old. The shrine is believed to offer good fortune, good harvests, and victory in sports.<ref name=":1" /> * ''Kuzuryu-sha'' (九頭龍社) literally means "nine-headed dragon shrine." It is dedicated to [[:en:Kuzuryū|Kuzuryu-no-Okami]], the deity of water, rain, and the prevention of [[:en:tooth decay|tooth decay]].<ref name=":1" />
There is a ''[[Shuin|goshuin]]'' stamp available at each of the five shrines.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=戸隠神社 {{!}} 霊山・戸隠山の麓を中心に創建された二千年余りに及ぶ歴史を刻む神社 |url=https://www.togakushi-jinja.jp/ |access-date=2024-08-14 |website=www.togakushi-jinja.jp |language=ja}}</ref> Many of the shrines are separated by several kilometers, so personal vehicles and regularly-scheduled buses can be used for transportation between shrines.<ref>{{Cite web |title=アクセス |url=https://www.togakushi-jinja.jp/access/ |access-date=2024-08-20 |website=www.togakushi-jinja.jp |language=ja}}</ref>
It is the [[Sōja shrine]] of [[:en:Kazusa Province|Kazusa Province]].

Until that time, it was common in Japan for the same buildings to be used as both temples (Buddhist) and shrines (Shinto). Until the 19th century, Buddhist activities at the Togakushi Temple were dedicated to "[[:en:Avalokiteśvara|Avalokiteśvara]]".

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Togakushi Shrine]][[de:Togakushi-Schrein]][[en:Togakushi Shrine]][[fr:Togakushi-jinja]][[it:Santuario Togakushi]][[ja:戸隠神社]][[zh:戶隱神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q218828]] Wikidata name: Togakushi Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:58, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nagano Prefecture, Japan}} {{more citations needed|date=June 2022}} {{cleanup rewrite|date=May 2013}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{Nihongo|'''Togakushi Shrine'''|戸隠神社|Togakushi Jinja}} | image = File:160430 Togakushi-jinja Chusha Nagano Japan02n.jpg | caption = The Lower Shrine, Hōkō-sha | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|36.742504|N|138.085128|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity ={{ill|Ame-no-tajikarao|WD=Q849412}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[:en:Togakushi, Nagano|Togakushi]], [[:en:Nagano City|Nagano City]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | prefecture = {{ill|Nagano|WD=Q128849}} | leadership = }} [[File:160430 Togakushi-jinja Chusha Nagano Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|The middle shrine Chū-sha]] [[File:Togakusi okusya.jpg|thumb|The upper shrine Oku-sha]] [[File:Tree lined path to the Togakushi shrine1.jpg|thumb|220x220px|Planted in the 17th century, the cedar-lined approach to the upper shrine Oku-sha]] The {{Nihongo|'''Togakushi Shrine'''|戸隠神社|Togakushi Jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Togakushi, Nagano|Togakushi]], [[:en:Nagano (city)|Nagano City]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The shrine is at the base of [[:en:Mount Togakushi|Mount Togakushi]] ({{Convert|1904|m|ft}}) in [[Myōkō-Togakushi Renzan National Park]].<ref>{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru Daijisen | title = Togakushiyama | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-12-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | oclc = 56431036 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }}</ref>
== Shrines == Togakushi Shrine consists of five shrines that can be visited in any order:
* The Lower Shrine (宝光社, ''Hōkō-sha'') enshrines the deity Ame-no-Uwaharu-no-Mikoto. It is believed to offer blessings related to academic life, [[:en:sewing|sewing]], safe [[:en:Childbirth|delivery]], and the protection of women and children.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=戸隠神社 {{!}} 霊山・戸隠山の麓を中心に創建された二千年余りに及ぶ歴史を刻む神社 |url=https://www.togakushi-jinja.jp/english/ |access-date=2024-08-20 |website=www.togakushi-jinja.jp |language=ja}}</ref> * ''Hinomiko-sha'' (火之御子社) enshrines four deities with the main deity being [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]]. There are two 500 year old [[:en:Cryptomeria|Japanese cedars]] known as the "husband and wife cedars" (夫婦の杉, ''Fufu-no-Sugi)'' and a famous [[:en:Cherry blossom|Cherry blossom]] tree known as [[:en:Saigyō|Saigyo]] Zakura. The shrine is believed to offer blessings for marriage, love, fire prevention, and entertainment.<ref name=":0" /> * The Middle Shrine (中社, ''Chū-sha'') has the head office of Togakushi Shrine. It enshrines [[Omoikane|Ame-no-Yagokoro-Omoikane-no-Mikoto]] and has several Japanese cedar trees ranging from 700 to 800 years old. It offers blessings for things such as academic achievement and business prosperity.<ref name=":0" /> * The Upper Shrine (奥社, ''Oku-sha'') enshrines Ame-no-Tachikarao-no-Mikoto. The approach to the shrine is about 2 kilometers long.<ref name=":1" /> The path to the shrine is lined with 400 year old Japanese cedars which are designated as [[Monuments of Japan|Natural Monuments of Japan]].<ref name=":1" /> About halfway through the approach to the shrine, there is a red gate called ''Zuishinmon'' (随神門). The shrine is believed to offer good fortune, good harvests, and victory in sports.<ref name=":1" /> * ''Kuzuryu-sha'' (九頭龍社) literally means "nine-headed dragon shrine." It is dedicated to [[:en:Kuzuryū|Kuzuryu-no-Okami]], the deity of water, rain, and the prevention of [[:en:tooth decay|tooth decay]].<ref name=":1" />
There is a ''[[Shuin|goshuin]]'' stamp available at each of the five shrines.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=戸隠神社 {{!}} 霊山・戸隠山の麓を中心に創建された二千年余りに及ぶ歴史を刻む神社 |url=https://www.togakushi-jinja.jp/ |access-date=2024-08-14 |website=www.togakushi-jinja.jp |language=ja}}</ref> Many of the shrines are separated by several kilometers, so personal vehicles and regularly-scheduled buses can be used for transportation between shrines.<ref>{{Cite web |title=アクセス |url=https://www.togakushi-jinja.jp/access/ |access-date=2024-08-20 |website=www.togakushi-jinja.jp |language=ja}}</ref>
It is the [[Sōja shrine]] of [[:en:Kazusa Province|Kazusa Province]].
==History== In one theory, the upper shrine, or ''Oku-sha'', is said to have been first constructed in the 5th year of the Emperor Kogen (210 BC) while Buddhist tradition holds that a monk named Gakumon discovered the Oku-sha area and began the practice of [[Shugendō|Shugendo]] there in the 2nd year of the Kasha era (849 AD). According to the [[:en:Nihon-Shoki|Nihon-Shoki]], the [[:en:Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]] had a map of the area made in 684 AD and a temporary building built the following year.
Togakushi shrine was a pilgrimage site during the following eight centuries. Its name was Ranked with the [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise-jingu]] Shrine, [[:en:Mount Kōya|Koya-san]] Temple and [[Enryaku-ji]] temples. Togakushisan Kansyuin Kenkou-ji was the formal name of the Togakushi Temple.
Two major esoteric Buddhist sects, [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]] and [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] fought for the hegemony of Togakushi Temple. Eventually the Shingon sect lost the battle. Togakushi Temple was changed to a shrine by the Meiji government's Buddhism/Shinto separation initiatives "[[Shinbutsu bunri]]", "[[Haibutsu kishaku]]", and the 1868 Temple Ordinance. Until that time, it was common in Japan for the same buildings to be used as both temples (Buddhist) and shrines (Shinto). Until the 19th century, Buddhist activities at the Togakushi Temple were dedicated to "[[:en:Avalokiteśvara|Avalokiteśvara]]".
== Site == Upon arrival at Togakushi it is recommended to first visit Oku-sha and then Kuzuryu-sha. It is a 2 kilometer hike from the entrance to the two shrines, however the path leading deep into the mountain can only be taken on foot. Beyond the cedar-lined path, you will be able to see the ''torii'' gate for Oku-sha at the bottom of the mountain and the ''shaden'' main building of the shrine as well. During winter the paths are closed but snowshoeing may be possible.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://naganonow.com/explore/togakushi-village-shrine/|title = Togakushi Village | Togakushi Shrine | Togakushi Ninja |Togakushi Ski Field|date = 17 December 2019}}</ref>

According to the [[:en:Nihon-Shoki|Nihon-Shoki]], the [[:en:Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]] had a map of the area made in 684 AD and a temporary building built the following year.

Two major esoteric Buddhist sects, [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]] and [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] fought for the hegemony of Togakushi Temple. Eventually the Shingon sect lost the battle. Togakushi Temple was changed to a shrine by the Meiji government's Buddhism/Shinto separation initiatives "[[Shinbutsu bunri]]", "[[Haibutsu kishaku]]", and the 1868 Temple Ordinance. Until that time, it was common in Japan for the same buildings to be used as both temples (Buddhist) and shrines (Shinto). Until the 19th century, Buddhist activities at the Togakushi Temple were dedicated to "[[:en:Avalokiteśvara|Avalokiteśvara]]".
== Site == Upon arrival at Togakushi it is recommended to first visit Oku-sha and then Kuzuryu-sha. It is a 2 kilometer hike from the entrance to the two shrines, however the path leading deep into the mountain can only be taken on foot. Beyond the cedar-lined path, you will be able to see the ''torii'' gate for Oku-sha at the bottom of the mountain and the ''shaden'' main building of the shrine as well. During winter the paths are closed but snowshoeing may be possible.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://naganonow.com/explore/togakushi-village-shrine/|title = Togakushi Village | Togakushi Shrine | Togakushi Ninja |Togakushi Ski Field|date = 17 December 2019}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Togakushi Shrine]][[de:Togakushi-Schrein]][[en:Togakushi Shrine]][[fr:Togakushi-jinja]][[it:Santuario Togakushi]][[ja:戸隠神社]][[zh:戶隱神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q218828]] Wikidata name: Togakushi Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:58, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nagano Prefecture, Japan}} {{more citations needed|date=June 2022}} {{cleanup rewrite|date=May 2013}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{Nihongo|'''Togakushi Shrine'''|戸隠神社|Togakushi Jinja}} | image = File:160430 Togakushi-jinja Chusha Nagano Japan02n.jpg | caption = The Lower Shrine, Hōkō-sha | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|36.742504|N|138.085128|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity ={{ill|Ame-no-tajikarao|WD=Q849412}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[:en:Togakushi, Nagano|Togakushi]], [[:en:Nagano City|Nagano City]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | prefecture = {{ill|Nagano|WD=Q128849}} | leadership = }} [[File:160430 Togakushi-jinja Chusha Nagano Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|The middle shrine Chū-sha]] [[File:Togakusi okusya.jpg|thumb|The upper shrine Oku-sha]] [[File:Tree lined path to the Togakushi shrine1.jpg|thumb|220x220px|Planted in the 17th century, the cedar-lined approach to the upper shrine Oku-sha]] The {{Nihongo|'''Togakushi Shrine'''|戸隠神社|Togakushi Jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Togakushi, Nagano|Togakushi]], [[:en:Nagano (city)|Nagano City]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The shrine is at the base of [[:en:Mount Togakushi|Mount Togakushi]] ({{Convert|1904|m|ft}}) in [[Myōkō-Togakushi Renzan National Park]].<ref>{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru Daijisen | title = Togakushiyama | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-12-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | oclc = 56431036 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }}</ref>
== Shrines == Togakushi Shrine consists of five shrines that can be visited in any order:
* The Lower Shrine (宝光社, ''Hōkō-sha'') enshrines the deity Ame-no-Uwaharu-no-Mikoto. It is believed to offer blessings related to academic life, [[:en:sewing|sewing]], safe [[:en:Childbirth|delivery]], and the protection of women and children.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=戸隠神社 {{!}} 霊山・戸隠山の麓を中心に創建された二千年余りに及ぶ歴史を刻む神社 |url=https://www.togakushi-jinja.jp/english/ |access-date=2024-08-20 |website=www.togakushi-jinja.jp |language=ja}}</ref> * ''Hinomiko-sha'' (火之御子社) enshrines four deities with the main deity being [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]]. There are two 500 year old [[:en:Cryptomeria|Japanese cedars]] known as the "husband and wife cedars" (夫婦の杉, ''Fufu-no-Sugi)'' and a famous [[:en:Cherry blossom|Cherry blossom]] tree known as [[:en:Saigyō|Saigyo]] Zakura. The shrine is believed to offer blessings for marriage, love, fire prevention, and entertainment.<ref name=":0" /> * The Middle Shrine (中社, ''Chū-sha'') has the head office of Togakushi Shrine. It enshrines [[Omoikane|Ame-no-Yagokoro-Omoikane-no-Mikoto]] and has several Japanese cedar trees ranging from 700 to 800 years old. It offers blessings for things such as academic achievement and business prosperity.<ref name=":0" /> * The Upper Shrine (奥社, ''Oku-sha'') enshrines Ame-no-Tachikarao-no-Mikoto. The approach to the shrine is about 2 kilometers long.<ref name=":1" /> The path to the shrine is lined with 400 year old Japanese cedars which are designated as [[Monuments of Japan|Natural Monuments of Japan]].<ref name=":1" /> About halfway through the approach to the shrine, there is a red gate called ''Zuishinmon'' (随神門). The shrine is believed to offer good fortune, good harvests, and victory in sports.<ref name=":1" /> * ''Kuzuryu-sha'' (九頭龍社) literally means "nine-headed dragon shrine." It is dedicated to [[:en:Kuzuryū|Kuzuryu-no-Okami]], the deity of water, rain, and the prevention of [[:en:tooth decay|tooth decay]].<ref name=":1" />
There is a ''[[Shuin|goshuin]]'' stamp available at each of the five shrines.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=戸隠神社 {{!}} 霊山・戸隠山の麓を中心に創建された二千年余りに及ぶ歴史を刻む神社 |url=https://www.togakushi-jinja.jp/ |access-date=2024-08-14 |website=www.togakushi-jinja.jp |language=ja}}</ref> Many of the shrines are separated by several kilometers, so personal vehicles and regularly-scheduled buses can be used for transportation between shrines.<ref>{{Cite web |title=アクセス |url=https://www.togakushi-jinja.jp/access/ |access-date=2024-08-20 |website=www.togakushi-jinja.jp |language=ja}}</ref>
It is the [[Sōja shrine]] of [[:en:Kazusa Province|Kazusa Province]].
==History== In one theory, the upper shrine, or ''Oku-sha'', is said to have been first constructed in the 5th year of the Emperor Kogen (210 BC) while Buddhist tradition holds that a monk named Gakumon discovered the Oku-sha area and began the practice of [[Shugendō|Shugendo]] there in the 2nd year of the Kasha era (849 AD). According to the [[:en:Nihon-Shoki|Nihon-Shoki]], the [[:en:Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]] had a map of the area made in 684 AD and a temporary building built the following year.
Togakushi shrine was a pilgrimage site during the following eight centuries. Its name was Ranked with the [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise-jingu]] Shrine, [[:en:Mount Kōya|Koya-san]] Temple and [[Enryaku-ji]] temples. Togakushisan Kansyuin Kenkou-ji was the formal name of the Togakushi Temple.
Two major esoteric Buddhist sects, [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]] and [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] fought for the hegemony of Togakushi Temple. Eventually the Shingon sect lost the battle. Togakushi Temple was changed to a shrine by the Meiji government's Buddhism/Shinto separation initiatives "[[Shinbutsu bunri]]", "[[Haibutsu kishaku]]", and the 1868 Temple Ordinance. Until that time, it was common in Japan for the same buildings to be used as both temples (Buddhist) and shrines (Shinto). Until the 19th century, Buddhist activities at the Togakushi Temple were dedicated to "[[:en:Avalokiteśvara|Avalokiteśvara]]".
== Site == Upon arrival at Togakushi it is recommended to first visit Oku-sha and then Kuzuryu-sha. It is a 2 kilometer hike from the entrance to the two shrines, however the path leading deep into the mountain can only be taken on foot. Beyond the cedar-lined path, you will be able to see the ''torii'' gate for Oku-sha at the bottom of the mountain and the ''shaden'' main building of the shrine as well. During winter the paths are closed but snowshoeing may be possible.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://naganonow.com/explore/togakushi-village-shrine/|title = Togakushi Village | Togakushi Shrine | Togakushi Ninja |Togakushi Ski Field|date = 17 December 2019}}</ref>

== Shrines == Togakushi Shrine consists of five shrines that can be visited in any order:
* The Lower Shrine (宝光社, ''Hōkō-sha'') enshrines the deity Ame-no-Uwaharu-no-Mikoto. It is believed to offer blessings related to academic life, [[:en:sewing|sewing]], safe [[:en:Childbirth|delivery]], and the protection of women and children.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=戸隠神社 {{!}} 霊山・戸隠山の麓を中心に創建された二千年余りに及ぶ歴史を刻む神社 |url=https://www.togakushi-jinja.jp/english/ |access-date=2024-08-20 |website=www.togakushi-jinja.jp |language=ja}}</ref> * ''Hinomiko-sha'' (火之御子社) enshrines four deities with the main deity being [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]]. There are two 500 year old [[:en:Cryptomeria|Japanese cedars]] known as the "husband and wife cedars" (夫婦の杉, ''Fufu-no-Sugi)'' and a famous [[:en:Cherry blossom|Cherry blossom]] tree known as [[:en:Saigyō|Saigyo]] Zakura. The shrine is believed to offer blessings for marriage, love, fire prevention, and entertainment.<ref name=":0" /> * The Middle Shrine (中社, ''Chū-sha'') has the head office of Togakushi Shrine. It enshrines [[Omoikane|Ame-no-Yagokoro-Omoikane-no-Mikoto]] and has several Japanese cedar trees ranging from 700 to 800 years old. It offers blessings for things such as academic achievement and business prosperity.<ref name=":0" /> * The Upper Shrine (奥社, ''Oku-sha'') enshrines Ame-no-Tachikarao-no-Mikoto. The approach to the shrine is about 2 kilometers long.<ref name=":1" /> The path to the shrine is lined with 400 year old Japanese cedars which are designated as [[Monuments of Japan|Natural Monuments of Japan]].<ref name=":1" /> About halfway through the approach to the shrine, there is a red gate called ''Zuishinmon'' (随神門). The shrine is believed to offer good fortune, good harvests, and victory in sports.<ref name=":1" /> * ''Kuzuryu-sha'' (九頭龍社) literally means "nine-headed dragon shrine." It is dedicated to [[:en:Kuzuryū|Kuzuryu-no-Okami]], the deity of water, rain, and the prevention of [[:en:tooth decay|tooth decay]].<ref name=":1" />
There is a ''[[Shuin|goshuin]]'' stamp available at each of the five shrines.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=戸隠神社 {{!}} 霊山・戸隠山の麓を中心に創建された二千年余りに及ぶ歴史を刻む神社 |url=https://www.togakushi-jinja.jp/ |access-date=2024-08-14 |website=www.togakushi-jinja.jp |language=ja}}</ref> Many of the shrines are separated by several kilometers, so personal vehicles and regularly-scheduled buses can be used for transportation between shrines.<ref>{{Cite web |title=アクセス |url=https://www.togakushi-jinja.jp/access/ |access-date=2024-08-20 |website=www.togakushi-jinja.jp |language=ja}}</ref>
It is the [[Sōja shrine]] of [[:en:Kazusa Province|Kazusa Province]].
==History== In one theory, the upper shrine, or ''Oku-sha'', is said to have been first constructed in the 5th year of the Emperor Kogen (210 BC) while Buddhist tradition holds that a monk named Gakumon discovered the Oku-sha area and began the practice of [[Shugendō|Shugendo]] there in the 2nd year of the Kasha era (849 AD). According to the [[:en:Nihon-Shoki|Nihon-Shoki]], the [[:en:Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]] had a map of the area made in 684 AD and a temporary building built the following year.
Togakushi shrine was a pilgrimage site during the following eight centuries. Its name was Ranked with the [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise-jingu]] Shrine, [[:en:Mount Kōya|Koya-san]] Temple and [[Enryaku-ji]] temples. Togakushisan Kansyuin Kenkou-ji was the formal name of the Togakushi Temple.
Two major esoteric Buddhist sects, [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]] and [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] fought for the hegemony of Togakushi Temple. Eventually the Shingon sect lost the battle. Togakushi Temple was changed to a shrine by the Meiji government's Buddhism/Shinto separation initiatives "[[Shinbutsu bunri]]", "[[Haibutsu kishaku]]", and the 1868 Temple Ordinance. Until that time, it was common in Japan for the same buildings to be used as both temples (Buddhist) and shrines (Shinto). Until the 19th century, Buddhist activities at the Togakushi Temple were dedicated to "[[:en:Avalokiteśvara|Avalokiteśvara]]".
== Site == Upon arrival at Togakushi it is recommended to first visit Oku-sha and then Kuzuryu-sha. It is a 2 kilometer hike from the entrance to the two shrines, however the path leading deep into the mountain can only be taken on foot. Beyond the cedar-lined path, you will be able to see the ''torii'' gate for Oku-sha at the bottom of the mountain and the ''shaden'' main building of the shrine as well. During winter the paths are closed but snowshoeing may be possible.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://naganonow.com/explore/togakushi-village-shrine/|title = Togakushi Village | Togakushi Shrine | Togakushi Ninja |Togakushi Ski Field|date = 17 December 2019}}</ref>

The shrine is at the base of [[:en:Mount Togakushi|Mount Togakushi]] ({{Convert|1904|m|ft}}) in [[Myōkō-Togakushi Renzan National Park]].<ref>{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru Daijisen | title = Togakushiyama | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-12-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | oclc = 56431036 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Togakushi Shrine]][[de:Togakushi-Schrein]][[en:Togakushi Shrine]][[fr:Togakushi-jinja]][[it:Santuario Togakushi]][[ja:戸隠神社]][[zh:戶隱神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q218828]] Wikidata name: Togakushi Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:58, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nagano Prefecture, Japan}} {{more citations needed|date=June 2022}} {{cleanup rewrite|date=May 2013}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{Nihongo|'''Togakushi Shrine'''|戸隠神社|Togakushi Jinja}} | image = File:160430 Togakushi-jinja Chusha Nagano Japan02n.jpg | caption = The Lower Shrine, Hōkō-sha | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|36.742504|N|138.085128|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity ={{ill|Ame-no-tajikarao|WD=Q849412}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[:en:Togakushi, Nagano|Togakushi]], [[:en:Nagano City|Nagano City]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | prefecture = {{ill|Nagano|WD=Q128849}} | leadership = }} [[File:160430 Togakushi-jinja Chusha Nagano Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|The middle shrine Chū-sha]] [[File:Togakusi okusya.jpg|thumb|The upper shrine Oku-sha]] [[File:Tree lined path to the Togakushi shrine1.jpg|thumb|220x220px|Planted in the 17th century, the cedar-lined approach to the upper shrine Oku-sha]] The {{Nihongo|'''Togakushi Shrine'''|戸隠神社|Togakushi Jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Togakushi, Nagano|Togakushi]], [[:en:Nagano (city)|Nagano City]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The shrine is at the base of [[:en:Mount Togakushi|Mount Togakushi]] ({{Convert|1904|m|ft}}) in [[Myōkō-Togakushi Renzan National Park]].<ref>{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru Daijisen | title = Togakushiyama | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-12-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | oclc = 56431036 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }}</ref>
== Shrines == Togakushi Shrine consists of five shrines that can be visited in any order:
* The Lower Shrine (宝光社, ''Hōkō-sha'') enshrines the deity Ame-no-Uwaharu-no-Mikoto. It is believed to offer blessings related to academic life, [[:en:sewing|sewing]], safe [[:en:Childbirth|delivery]], and the protection of women and children.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=戸隠神社 {{!}} 霊山・戸隠山の麓を中心に創建された二千年余りに及ぶ歴史を刻む神社 |url=https://www.togakushi-jinja.jp/english/ |access-date=2024-08-20 |website=www.togakushi-jinja.jp |language=ja}}</ref> * ''Hinomiko-sha'' (火之御子社) enshrines four deities with the main deity being [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]]. There are two 500 year old [[:en:Cryptomeria|Japanese cedars]] known as the "husband and wife cedars" (夫婦の杉, ''Fufu-no-Sugi)'' and a famous [[:en:Cherry blossom|Cherry blossom]] tree known as [[:en:Saigyō|Saigyo]] Zakura. The shrine is believed to offer blessings for marriage, love, fire prevention, and entertainment.<ref name=":0" /> * The Middle Shrine (中社, ''Chū-sha'') has the head office of Togakushi Shrine. It enshrines [[Omoikane|Ame-no-Yagokoro-Omoikane-no-Mikoto]] and has several Japanese cedar trees ranging from 700 to 800 years old. It offers blessings for things such as academic achievement and business prosperity.<ref name=":0" /> * The Upper Shrine (奥社, ''Oku-sha'') enshrines Ame-no-Tachikarao-no-Mikoto. The approach to the shrine is about 2 kilometers long.<ref name=":1" /> The path to the shrine is lined with 400 year old Japanese cedars which are designated as [[Monuments of Japan|Natural Monuments of Japan]].<ref name=":1" /> About halfway through the approach to the shrine, there is a red gate called ''Zuishinmon'' (随神門). The shrine is believed to offer good fortune, good harvests, and victory in sports.<ref name=":1" /> * ''Kuzuryu-sha'' (九頭龍社) literally means "nine-headed dragon shrine." It is dedicated to [[:en:Kuzuryū|Kuzuryu-no-Okami]], the deity of water, rain, and the prevention of [[:en:tooth decay|tooth decay]].<ref name=":1" />
There is a ''[[Shuin|goshuin]]'' stamp available at each of the five shrines.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=戸隠神社 {{!}} 霊山・戸隠山の麓を中心に創建された二千年余りに及ぶ歴史を刻む神社 |url=https://www.togakushi-jinja.jp/ |access-date=2024-08-14 |website=www.togakushi-jinja.jp |language=ja}}</ref> Many of the shrines are separated by several kilometers, so personal vehicles and regularly-scheduled buses can be used for transportation between shrines.<ref>{{Cite web |title=アクセス |url=https://www.togakushi-jinja.jp/access/ |access-date=2024-08-20 |website=www.togakushi-jinja.jp |language=ja}}</ref>
It is the [[Sōja shrine]] of [[:en:Kazusa Province|Kazusa Province]].
==History== In one theory, the upper shrine, or ''Oku-sha'', is said to have been first constructed in the 5th year of the Emperor Kogen (210 BC) while Buddhist tradition holds that a monk named Gakumon discovered the Oku-sha area and began the practice of [[Shugendō|Shugendo]] there in the 2nd year of the Kasha era (849 AD). According to the [[:en:Nihon-Shoki|Nihon-Shoki]], the [[:en:Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]] had a map of the area made in 684 AD and a temporary building built the following year.
Togakushi shrine was a pilgrimage site during the following eight centuries. Its name was Ranked with the [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise-jingu]] Shrine, [[:en:Mount Kōya|Koya-san]] Temple and [[Enryaku-ji]] temples. Togakushisan Kansyuin Kenkou-ji was the formal name of the Togakushi Temple.
Two major esoteric Buddhist sects, [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]] and [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] fought for the hegemony of Togakushi Temple. Eventually the Shingon sect lost the battle. Togakushi Temple was changed to a shrine by the Meiji government's Buddhism/Shinto separation initiatives "[[Shinbutsu bunri]]", "[[Haibutsu kishaku]]", and the 1868 Temple Ordinance. Until that time, it was common in Japan for the same buildings to be used as both temples (Buddhist) and shrines (Shinto). Until the 19th century, Buddhist activities at the Togakushi Temple were dedicated to "[[:en:Avalokiteśvara|Avalokiteśvara]]".
== Site == Upon arrival at Togakushi it is recommended to first visit Oku-sha and then Kuzuryu-sha. It is a 2 kilometer hike from the entrance to the two shrines, however the path leading deep into the mountain can only be taken on foot. Beyond the cedar-lined path, you will be able to see the ''torii'' gate for Oku-sha at the bottom of the mountain and the ''shaden'' main building of the shrine as well. During winter the paths are closed but snowshoeing may be possible.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://naganonow.com/explore/togakushi-village-shrine/|title = Togakushi Village | Togakushi Shrine | Togakushi Ninja |Togakushi Ski Field|date = 17 December 2019}}</ref>

There is a ''[[Shuin|goshuin]]'' stamp available at each of the five shrines.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=戸隠神社 {{!}} 霊山・戸隠山の麓を中心に創建された二千年余りに及ぶ歴史を刻む神社 |url=https://www.togakushi-jinja.jp/ |access-date=2024-08-14 |website=www.togakushi-jinja.jp |language=ja}}</ref> Many of the shrines are separated by several kilometers, so personal vehicles and regularly-scheduled buses can be used for transportation between shrines.<ref>{{Cite web |title=アクセス |url=https://www.togakushi-jinja.jp/access/ |access-date=2024-08-20 |website=www.togakushi-jinja.jp |language=ja}}</ref>
It is the [[Sōja shrine]] of [[:en:Kazusa Province|Kazusa Province]].
==History== In one theory, the upper shrine, or ''Oku-sha'', is said to have been first constructed in the 5th year of the Emperor Kogen (210 BC) while Buddhist tradition holds that a monk named Gakumon discovered the Oku-sha area and began the practice of [[Shugendō|Shugendo]] there in the 2nd year of the Kasha era (849 AD). According to the [[:en:Nihon-Shoki|Nihon-Shoki]], the [[:en:Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]] had a map of the area made in 684 AD and a temporary building built the following year.
Togakushi shrine was a pilgrimage site during the following eight centuries. Its name was Ranked with the [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise-jingu]] Shrine, [[:en:Mount Kōya|Koya-san]] Temple and [[Enryaku-ji]] temples. Togakushisan Kansyuin Kenkou-ji was the formal name of the Togakushi Temple.
Two major esoteric Buddhist sects, [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]] and [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] fought for the hegemony of Togakushi Temple. Eventually the Shingon sect lost the battle. Togakushi Temple was changed to a shrine by the Meiji government's Buddhism/Shinto separation initiatives "[[Shinbutsu bunri]]", "[[Haibutsu kishaku]]", and the 1868 Temple Ordinance. Until that time, it was common in Japan for the same buildings to be used as both temples (Buddhist) and shrines (Shinto). Until the 19th century, Buddhist activities at the Togakushi Temple were dedicated to "[[:en:Avalokiteśvara|Avalokiteśvara]]".
== Site == Upon arrival at Togakushi it is recommended to first visit Oku-sha and then Kuzuryu-sha. It is a 2 kilometer hike from the entrance to the two shrines, however the path leading deep into the mountain can only be taken on foot. Beyond the cedar-lined path, you will be able to see the ''torii'' gate for Oku-sha at the bottom of the mountain and the ''shaden'' main building of the shrine as well. During winter the paths are closed but snowshoeing may be possible.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://naganonow.com/explore/togakushi-village-shrine/|title = Togakushi Village | Togakushi Shrine | Togakushi Ninja |Togakushi Ski Field|date = 17 December 2019}}</ref>
== See also == * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] * {{ill|Iigaoka Hachimangu|ja|飯香岡八幡宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iigaoka Hachimangu|qq=}} (the other [[Sōja shrine]] of [[:en:Kazusa Province|Kazusa Province]]) * [[Tamasaki Shrine]] ([[:en:Kazusa Province|Kazusa Province]] [[Ichinomiya]])

The shrine is at the base of [[:en:Mount Togakushi|Mount Togakushi]] ({{Convert|1904|m|ft}}) in [[Myōkō-Togakushi Renzan National Park]].<ref>{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru Daijisen | title = Togakushiyama | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-12-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | oclc = 56431036 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }}</ref>
== Shrines == Togakushi Shrine consists of five shrines that can be visited in any order:
* The Lower Shrine (宝光社, ''Hōkō-sha'') enshrines the deity Ame-no-Uwaharu-no-Mikoto. It is believed to offer blessings related to academic life, [[:en:sewing|sewing]], safe [[:en:Childbirth|delivery]], and the protection of women and children.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=戸隠神社 {{!}} 霊山・戸隠山の麓を中心に創建された二千年余りに及ぶ歴史を刻む神社 |url=https://www.togakushi-jinja.jp/english/ |access-date=2024-08-20 |website=www.togakushi-jinja.jp |language=ja}}</ref> * ''Hinomiko-sha'' (火之御子社) enshrines four deities with the main deity being [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]]. There are two 500 year old [[:en:Cryptomeria|Japanese cedars]] known as the "husband and wife cedars" (夫婦の杉, ''Fufu-no-Sugi)'' and a famous [[:en:Cherry blossom|Cherry blossom]] tree known as [[:en:Saigyō|Saigyo]] Zakura. The shrine is believed to offer blessings for marriage, love, fire prevention, and entertainment.<ref name=":0" /> * The Middle Shrine (中社, ''Chū-sha'') has the head office of Togakushi Shrine. It enshrines [[Omoikane|Ame-no-Yagokoro-Omoikane-no-Mikoto]] and has several Japanese cedar trees ranging from 700 to 800 years old. It offers blessings for things such as academic achievement and business prosperity.<ref name=":0" /> * The Upper Shrine (奥社, ''Oku-sha'') enshrines Ame-no-Tachikarao-no-Mikoto. The approach to the shrine is about 2 kilometers long.<ref name=":1" /> The path to the shrine is lined with 400 year old Japanese cedars which are designated as [[Monuments of Japan|Natural Monuments of Japan]].<ref name=":1" /> About halfway through the approach to the shrine, there is a red gate called ''Zuishinmon'' (随神門). The shrine is believed to offer good fortune, good harvests, and victory in sports.<ref name=":1" /> * ''Kuzuryu-sha'' (九頭龍社) literally means "nine-headed dragon shrine." It is dedicated to [[:en:Kuzuryū|Kuzuryu-no-Okami]], the deity of water, rain, and the prevention of [[:en:tooth decay|tooth decay]].<ref name=":1" />
There is a ''[[Shuin|goshuin]]'' stamp available at each of the five shrines.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=戸隠神社 {{!}} 霊山・戸隠山の麓を中心に創建された二千年余りに及ぶ歴史を刻む神社 |url=https://www.togakushi-jinja.jp/ |access-date=2024-08-14 |website=www.togakushi-jinja.jp |language=ja}}</ref> Many of the shrines are separated by several kilometers, so personal vehicles and regularly-scheduled buses can be used for transportation between shrines.<ref>{{Cite web |title=アクセス |url=https://www.togakushi-jinja.jp/access/ |access-date=2024-08-20 |website=www.togakushi-jinja.jp |language=ja}}</ref>
It is the [[Sōja shrine]] of [[:en:Kazusa Province|Kazusa Province]].
==History== In one theory, the upper shrine, or ''Oku-sha'', is said to have been first constructed in the 5th year of the Emperor Kogen (210 BC) while Buddhist tradition holds that a monk named Gakumon discovered the Oku-sha area and began the practice of [[Shugendō|Shugendo]] there in the 2nd year of the Kasha era (849 AD). According to the [[:en:Nihon-Shoki|Nihon-Shoki]], the [[:en:Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]] had a map of the area made in 684 AD and a temporary building built the following year.
Togakushi shrine was a pilgrimage site during the following eight centuries. Its name was Ranked with the [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise-jingu]] Shrine, [[:en:Mount Kōya|Koya-san]] Temple and [[Enryaku-ji]] temples. Togakushisan Kansyuin Kenkou-ji was the formal name of the Togakushi Temple.
Two major esoteric Buddhist sects, [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]] and [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] fought for the hegemony of Togakushi Temple. Eventually the Shingon sect lost the battle. Togakushi Temple was changed to a shrine by the Meiji government's Buddhism/Shinto separation initiatives "[[Shinbutsu bunri]]", "[[Haibutsu kishaku]]", and the 1868 Temple Ordinance. Until that time, it was common in Japan for the same buildings to be used as both temples (Buddhist) and shrines (Shinto). Until the 19th century, Buddhist activities at the Togakushi Temple were dedicated to "[[:en:Avalokiteśvara|Avalokiteśvara]]".
== Site == Upon arrival at Togakushi it is recommended to first visit Oku-sha and then Kuzuryu-sha. It is a 2 kilometer hike from the entrance to the two shrines, however the path leading deep into the mountain can only be taken on foot. Beyond the cedar-lined path, you will be able to see the ''torii'' gate for Oku-sha at the bottom of the mountain and the ''shaden'' main building of the shrine as well. During winter the paths are closed but snowshoeing may be possible.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://naganonow.com/explore/togakushi-village-shrine/|title = Togakushi Village | Togakushi Shrine | Togakushi Ninja |Togakushi Ski Field|date = 17 December 2019}}</ref>
== See also == * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] * {{ill|Iigaoka Hachimangu|ja|飯香岡八幡宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iigaoka Hachimangu|qq=}} (the other [[Sōja shrine]] of [[:en:Kazusa Province|Kazusa Province]]) * [[Tamasaki Shrine]] ([[:en:Kazusa Province|Kazusa Province]] [[Ichinomiya]])

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Togakushi Shrine]][[de:Togakushi-Schrein]][[en:Togakushi Shrine]][[fr:Togakushi-jinja]][[it:Santuario Togakushi]][[ja:戸隠神社]][[zh:戶隱神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q218828]] Wikidata name: Togakushi Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:58, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nagano Prefecture, Japan}} {{more citations needed|date=June 2022}} {{cleanup rewrite|date=May 2013}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{Nihongo|'''Togakushi Shrine'''|戸隠神社|Togakushi Jinja}} | image = File:160430 Togakushi-jinja Chusha Nagano Japan02n.jpg | caption = The Lower Shrine, Hōkō-sha | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|36.742504|N|138.085128|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity ={{ill|Ame-no-tajikarao|WD=Q849412}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[:en:Togakushi, Nagano|Togakushi]], [[:en:Nagano City|Nagano City]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | prefecture = {{ill|Nagano|WD=Q128849}} | leadership = }} [[File:160430 Togakushi-jinja Chusha Nagano Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|The middle shrine Chū-sha]] [[File:Togakusi okusya.jpg|thumb|The upper shrine Oku-sha]] [[File:Tree lined path to the Togakushi shrine1.jpg|thumb|220x220px|Planted in the 17th century, the cedar-lined approach to the upper shrine Oku-sha]] The {{Nihongo|'''Togakushi Shrine'''|戸隠神社|Togakushi Jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Togakushi, Nagano|Togakushi]], [[:en:Nagano (city)|Nagano City]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The shrine is at the base of [[:en:Mount Togakushi|Mount Togakushi]] ({{Convert|1904|m|ft}}) in [[Myōkō-Togakushi Renzan National Park]].<ref>{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru Daijisen | title = Togakushiyama | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-12-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | oclc = 56431036 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }}</ref>
== Shrines == Togakushi Shrine consists of five shrines that can be visited in any order:
* The Lower Shrine (宝光社, ''Hōkō-sha'') enshrines the deity Ame-no-Uwaharu-no-Mikoto. It is believed to offer blessings related to academic life, [[:en:sewing|sewing]], safe [[:en:Childbirth|delivery]], and the protection of women and children.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=戸隠神社 {{!}} 霊山・戸隠山の麓を中心に創建された二千年余りに及ぶ歴史を刻む神社 |url=https://www.togakushi-jinja.jp/english/ |access-date=2024-08-20 |website=www.togakushi-jinja.jp |language=ja}}</ref> * ''Hinomiko-sha'' (火之御子社) enshrines four deities with the main deity being [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]]. There are two 500 year old [[:en:Cryptomeria|Japanese cedars]] known as the "husband and wife cedars" (夫婦の杉, ''Fufu-no-Sugi)'' and a famous [[:en:Cherry blossom|Cherry blossom]] tree known as [[:en:Saigyō|Saigyo]] Zakura. The shrine is believed to offer blessings for marriage, love, fire prevention, and entertainment.<ref name=":0" /> * The Middle Shrine (中社, ''Chū-sha'') has the head office of Togakushi Shrine. It enshrines [[Omoikane|Ame-no-Yagokoro-Omoikane-no-Mikoto]] and has several Japanese cedar trees ranging from 700 to 800 years old. It offers blessings for things such as academic achievement and business prosperity.<ref name=":0" /> * The Upper Shrine (奥社, ''Oku-sha'') enshrines Ame-no-Tachikarao-no-Mikoto. The approach to the shrine is about 2 kilometers long.<ref name=":1" /> The path to the shrine is lined with 400 year old Japanese cedars which are designated as [[Monuments of Japan|Natural Monuments of Japan]].<ref name=":1" /> About halfway through the approach to the shrine, there is a red gate called ''Zuishinmon'' (随神門). The shrine is believed to offer good fortune, good harvests, and victory in sports.<ref name=":1" /> * ''Kuzuryu-sha'' (九頭龍社) literally means "nine-headed dragon shrine." It is dedicated to [[:en:Kuzuryū|Kuzuryu-no-Okami]], the deity of water, rain, and the prevention of [[:en:tooth decay|tooth decay]].<ref name=":1" />
There is a ''[[Shuin|goshuin]]'' stamp available at each of the five shrines.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=戸隠神社 {{!}} 霊山・戸隠山の麓を中心に創建された二千年余りに及ぶ歴史を刻む神社 |url=https://www.togakushi-jinja.jp/ |access-date=2024-08-14 |website=www.togakushi-jinja.jp |language=ja}}</ref> Many of the shrines are separated by several kilometers, so personal vehicles and regularly-scheduled buses can be used for transportation between shrines.<ref>{{Cite web |title=アクセス |url=https://www.togakushi-jinja.jp/access/ |access-date=2024-08-20 |website=www.togakushi-jinja.jp |language=ja}}</ref>
It is the [[Sōja shrine]] of [[:en:Kazusa Province|Kazusa Province]].
==History== In one theory, the upper shrine, or ''Oku-sha'', is said to have been first constructed in the 5th year of the Emperor Kogen (210 BC) while Buddhist tradition holds that a monk named Gakumon discovered the Oku-sha area and began the practice of [[Shugendō|Shugendo]] there in the 2nd year of the Kasha era (849 AD). According to the [[:en:Nihon-Shoki|Nihon-Shoki]], the [[:en:Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]] had a map of the area made in 684 AD and a temporary building built the following year.
Togakushi shrine was a pilgrimage site during the following eight centuries. Its name was Ranked with the [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise-jingu]] Shrine, [[:en:Mount Kōya|Koya-san]] Temple and [[Enryaku-ji]] temples. Togakushisan Kansyuin Kenkou-ji was the formal name of the Togakushi Temple.
Two major esoteric Buddhist sects, [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]] and [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] fought for the hegemony of Togakushi Temple. Eventually the Shingon sect lost the battle. Togakushi Temple was changed to a shrine by the Meiji government's Buddhism/Shinto separation initiatives "[[Shinbutsu bunri]]", "[[Haibutsu kishaku]]", and the 1868 Temple Ordinance. Until that time, it was common in Japan for the same buildings to be used as both temples (Buddhist) and shrines (Shinto). Until the 19th century, Buddhist activities at the Togakushi Temple were dedicated to "[[:en:Avalokiteśvara|Avalokiteśvara]]".
== Site == Upon arrival at Togakushi it is recommended to first visit Oku-sha and then Kuzuryu-sha. It is a 2 kilometer hike from the entrance to the two shrines, however the path leading deep into the mountain can only be taken on foot. Beyond the cedar-lined path, you will be able to see the ''torii'' gate for Oku-sha at the bottom of the mountain and the ''shaden'' main building of the shrine as well. During winter the paths are closed but snowshoeing may be possible.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://naganonow.com/explore/togakushi-village-shrine/|title = Togakushi Village | Togakushi Shrine | Togakushi Ninja |Togakushi Ski Field|date = 17 December 2019}}</ref>
== See also == * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] * {{ill|Iigaoka Hachimangu|ja|飯香岡八幡宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iigaoka Hachimangu|qq=}} (the other [[Sōja shrine]] of [[:en:Kazusa Province|Kazusa Province]]) * [[Tamasaki Shrine]] ([[:en:Kazusa Province|Kazusa Province]] [[Ichinomiya]])
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Togakushi Shrine}} * [http://www.togakushi-jinja.jp/ 戸隠神社 (''Togakushi-jinja'') website] * [http://www.genbu.net/data/sinano/togakusi4_title.htm Togakushi-shrine's Pilgrimage stamp. gensyoushinokioku] in Japanese * [https://naganonow.com/explore/togakushi-village-shrine/ Togakushi Village | Togakushi Shrine] in English

According to the [[:en:Nihon-Shoki|Nihon-Shoki]], the [[:en:Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]] had a map of the area made in 684 AD and a temporary building built the following year.
Togakushi shrine was a pilgrimage site during the following eight centuries. Its name was Ranked with the [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise-jingu]] Shrine, [[:en:Mount Kōya|Koya-san]] Temple and [[Enryaku-ji]] temples. Togakushisan Kansyuin Kenkou-ji was the formal name of the Togakushi Temple.
Two major esoteric Buddhist sects, [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]] and [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] fought for the hegemony of Togakushi Temple. Eventually the Shingon sect lost the battle. Togakushi Temple was changed to a shrine by the Meiji government's Buddhism/Shinto separation initiatives "[[Shinbutsu bunri]]", "[[Haibutsu kishaku]]", and the 1868 Temple Ordinance. Until that time, it was common in Japan for the same buildings to be used as both temples (Buddhist) and shrines (Shinto). Until the 19th century, Buddhist activities at the Togakushi Temple were dedicated to "[[:en:Avalokiteśvara|Avalokiteśvara]]".
== Site == Upon arrival at Togakushi it is recommended to first visit Oku-sha and then Kuzuryu-sha. It is a 2 kilometer hike from the entrance to the two shrines, however the path leading deep into the mountain can only be taken on foot. Beyond the cedar-lined path, you will be able to see the ''torii'' gate for Oku-sha at the bottom of the mountain and the ''shaden'' main building of the shrine as well. During winter the paths are closed but snowshoeing may be possible.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://naganonow.com/explore/togakushi-village-shrine/|title = Togakushi Village | Togakushi Shrine | Togakushi Ninja |Togakushi Ski Field|date = 17 December 2019}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Togakushi Shrine]][[de:Togakushi-Schrein]][[en:Togakushi Shrine]][[fr:Togakushi-jinja]][[it:Santuario Togakushi]][[ja:戸隠神社]][[zh:戶隱神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q218828]] Wikidata name: Togakushi Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:58, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nagano Prefecture, Japan}} {{more citations needed|date=June 2022}} {{cleanup rewrite|date=May 2013}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{Nihongo|'''Togakushi Shrine'''|戸隠神社|Togakushi Jinja}} | image = File:160430 Togakushi-jinja Chusha Nagano Japan02n.jpg | caption = The Lower Shrine, Hōkō-sha | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|36.742504|N|138.085128|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity ={{ill|Ame-no-tajikarao|WD=Q849412}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[:en:Togakushi, Nagano|Togakushi]], [[:en:Nagano City|Nagano City]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | prefecture = {{ill|Nagano|WD=Q128849}} | leadership = }} [[File:160430 Togakushi-jinja Chusha Nagano Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|The middle shrine Chū-sha]] [[File:Togakusi okusya.jpg|thumb|The upper shrine Oku-sha]] [[File:Tree lined path to the Togakushi shrine1.jpg|thumb|220x220px|Planted in the 17th century, the cedar-lined approach to the upper shrine Oku-sha]] The {{Nihongo|'''Togakushi Shrine'''|戸隠神社|Togakushi Jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Togakushi, Nagano|Togakushi]], [[:en:Nagano (city)|Nagano City]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The shrine is at the base of [[:en:Mount Togakushi|Mount Togakushi]] ({{Convert|1904|m|ft}}) in [[Myōkō-Togakushi Renzan National Park]].<ref>{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru Daijisen | title = Togakushiyama | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-12-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | oclc = 56431036 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }}</ref>
== Shrines == Togakushi Shrine consists of five shrines that can be visited in any order:
* The Lower Shrine (宝光社, ''Hōkō-sha'') enshrines the deity Ame-no-Uwaharu-no-Mikoto. It is believed to offer blessings related to academic life, [[:en:sewing|sewing]], safe [[:en:Childbirth|delivery]], and the protection of women and children.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=戸隠神社 {{!}} 霊山・戸隠山の麓を中心に創建された二千年余りに及ぶ歴史を刻む神社 |url=https://www.togakushi-jinja.jp/english/ |access-date=2024-08-20 |website=www.togakushi-jinja.jp |language=ja}}</ref> * ''Hinomiko-sha'' (火之御子社) enshrines four deities with the main deity being [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]]. There are two 500 year old [[:en:Cryptomeria|Japanese cedars]] known as the "husband and wife cedars" (夫婦の杉, ''Fufu-no-Sugi)'' and a famous [[:en:Cherry blossom|Cherry blossom]] tree known as [[:en:Saigyō|Saigyo]] Zakura. The shrine is believed to offer blessings for marriage, love, fire prevention, and entertainment.<ref name=":0" /> * The Middle Shrine (中社, ''Chū-sha'') has the head office of Togakushi Shrine. It enshrines [[Omoikane|Ame-no-Yagokoro-Omoikane-no-Mikoto]] and has several Japanese cedar trees ranging from 700 to 800 years old. It offers blessings for things such as academic achievement and business prosperity.<ref name=":0" /> * The Upper Shrine (奥社, ''Oku-sha'') enshrines Ame-no-Tachikarao-no-Mikoto. The approach to the shrine is about 2 kilometers long.<ref name=":1" /> The path to the shrine is lined with 400 year old Japanese cedars which are designated as [[Monuments of Japan|Natural Monuments of Japan]].<ref name=":1" /> About halfway through the approach to the shrine, there is a red gate called ''Zuishinmon'' (随神門). The shrine is believed to offer good fortune, good harvests, and victory in sports.<ref name=":1" /> * ''Kuzuryu-sha'' (九頭龍社) literally means "nine-headed dragon shrine." It is dedicated to [[:en:Kuzuryū|Kuzuryu-no-Okami]], the deity of water, rain, and the prevention of [[:en:tooth decay|tooth decay]].<ref name=":1" />
There is a ''[[Shuin|goshuin]]'' stamp available at each of the five shrines.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=戸隠神社 {{!}} 霊山・戸隠山の麓を中心に創建された二千年余りに及ぶ歴史を刻む神社 |url=https://www.togakushi-jinja.jp/ |access-date=2024-08-14 |website=www.togakushi-jinja.jp |language=ja}}</ref> Many of the shrines are separated by several kilometers, so personal vehicles and regularly-scheduled buses can be used for transportation between shrines.<ref>{{Cite web |title=アクセス |url=https://www.togakushi-jinja.jp/access/ |access-date=2024-08-20 |website=www.togakushi-jinja.jp |language=ja}}</ref>
It is the [[Sōja shrine]] of [[:en:Kazusa Province|Kazusa Province]].
==History== In one theory, the upper shrine, or ''Oku-sha'', is said to have been first constructed in the 5th year of the Emperor Kogen (210 BC) while Buddhist tradition holds that a monk named Gakumon discovered the Oku-sha area and began the practice of [[Shugendō|Shugendo]] there in the 2nd year of the Kasha era (849 AD). According to the [[:en:Nihon-Shoki|Nihon-Shoki]], the [[:en:Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]] had a map of the area made in 684 AD and a temporary building built the following year.
Togakushi shrine was a pilgrimage site during the following eight centuries. Its name was Ranked with the [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise-jingu]] Shrine, [[:en:Mount Kōya|Koya-san]] Temple and [[Enryaku-ji]] temples. Togakushisan Kansyuin Kenkou-ji was the formal name of the Togakushi Temple.
Two major esoteric Buddhist sects, [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]] and [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] fought for the hegemony of Togakushi Temple. Eventually the Shingon sect lost the battle. Togakushi Temple was changed to a shrine by the Meiji government's Buddhism/Shinto separation initiatives "[[Shinbutsu bunri]]", "[[Haibutsu kishaku]]", and the 1868 Temple Ordinance. Until that time, it was common in Japan for the same buildings to be used as both temples (Buddhist) and shrines (Shinto). Until the 19th century, Buddhist activities at the Togakushi Temple were dedicated to "[[:en:Avalokiteśvara|Avalokiteśvara]]".
== Site == Upon arrival at Togakushi it is recommended to first visit Oku-sha and then Kuzuryu-sha. It is a 2 kilometer hike from the entrance to the two shrines, however the path leading deep into the mountain can only be taken on foot. Beyond the cedar-lined path, you will be able to see the ''torii'' gate for Oku-sha at the bottom of the mountain and the ''shaden'' main building of the shrine as well. During winter the paths are closed but snowshoeing may be possible.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://naganonow.com/explore/togakushi-village-shrine/|title = Togakushi Village | Togakushi Shrine | Togakushi Ninja |Togakushi Ski Field|date = 17 December 2019}}</ref>

{{Nihongo|'''Togakushi Shrine'''|戸隠神社|Togakushi Jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Togakushi, Nagano|Togakushi]], [[:en:Nagano (city)|Nagano City]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The shrine is at the base of [[:en:Mount Togakushi|Mount Togakushi]] ({{Convert|1904|m|ft}}) in [[Myōkō-Togakushi Renzan National Park]].<ref>{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru Daijisen | title = Togakushiyama | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-12-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | oclc = 56431036 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }}</ref>
== Shrines == Togakushi Shrine consists of five shrines that can be visited in any order:
* The Lower Shrine (宝光社, ''Hōkō-sha'') enshrines the deity Ame-no-Uwaharu-no-Mikoto. It is believed to offer blessings related to academic life, [[:en:sewing|sewing]], safe [[:en:Childbirth|delivery]], and the protection of women and children.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=戸隠神社 {{!}} 霊山・戸隠山の麓を中心に創建された二千年余りに及ぶ歴史を刻む神社 |url=https://www.togakushi-jinja.jp/english/ |access-date=2024-08-20 |website=www.togakushi-jinja.jp |language=ja}}</ref> * ''Hinomiko-sha'' (火之御子社) enshrines four deities with the main deity being [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]]. There are two 500 year old [[:en:Cryptomeria|Japanese cedars]] known as the "husband and wife cedars" (夫婦の杉, ''Fufu-no-Sugi)'' and a famous [[:en:Cherry blossom|Cherry blossom]] tree known as [[:en:Saigyō|Saigyo]] Zakura. The shrine is believed to offer blessings for marriage, love, fire prevention, and entertainment.<ref name=":0" /> * The Middle Shrine (中社, ''Chū-sha'') has the head office of Togakushi Shrine. It enshrines [[Omoikane|Ame-no-Yagokoro-Omoikane-no-Mikoto]] and has several Japanese cedar trees ranging from 700 to 800 years old. It offers blessings for things such as academic achievement and business prosperity.<ref name=":0" /> * The Upper Shrine (奥社, ''Oku-sha'') enshrines Ame-no-Tachikarao-no-Mikoto. The approach to the shrine is about 2 kilometers long.<ref name=":1" /> The path to the shrine is lined with 400 year old Japanese cedars which are designated as [[Monuments of Japan|Natural Monuments of Japan]].<ref name=":1" /> About halfway through the approach to the shrine, there is a red gate called ''Zuishinmon'' (随神門). The shrine is believed to offer good fortune, good harvests, and victory in sports.<ref name=":1" /> * ''Kuzuryu-sha'' (九頭龍社) literally means "nine-headed dragon shrine." It is dedicated to [[:en:Kuzuryū|Kuzuryu-no-Okami]], the deity of water, rain, and the prevention of [[:en:tooth decay|tooth decay]].<ref name=":1" />
There is a ''[[Shuin|goshuin]]'' stamp available at each of the five shrines.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=戸隠神社 {{!}} 霊山・戸隠山の麓を中心に創建された二千年余りに及ぶ歴史を刻む神社 |url=https://www.togakushi-jinja.jp/ |access-date=2024-08-14 |website=www.togakushi-jinja.jp |language=ja}}</ref> Many of the shrines are separated by several kilometers, so personal vehicles and regularly-scheduled buses can be used for transportation between shrines.<ref>{{Cite web |title=アクセス |url=https://www.togakushi-jinja.jp/access/ |access-date=2024-08-20 |website=www.togakushi-jinja.jp |language=ja}}</ref>
It is the [[Sōja shrine]] of [[:en:Kazusa Province|Kazusa Province]].
==History== In one theory, the upper shrine, or ''Oku-sha'', is said to have been first constructed in the 5th year of the Emperor Kogen (210 BC) while Buddhist tradition holds that a monk named Gakumon discovered the Oku-sha area and began the practice of [[Shugendō|Shugendo]] there in the 2nd year of the Kasha era (849 AD). According to the [[:en:Nihon-Shoki|Nihon-Shoki]], the [[:en:Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]] had a map of the area made in 684 AD and a temporary building built the following year.
Togakushi shrine was a pilgrimage site during the following eight centuries. Its name was Ranked with the [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise-jingu]] Shrine, [[:en:Mount Kōya|Koya-san]] Temple and [[Enryaku-ji]] temples. Togakushisan Kansyuin Kenkou-ji was the formal name of the Togakushi Temple.
Two major esoteric Buddhist sects, [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]] and [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] fought for the hegemony of Togakushi Temple. Eventually the Shingon sect lost the battle. Togakushi Temple was changed to a shrine by the Meiji government's Buddhism/Shinto separation initiatives "[[Shinbutsu bunri]]", "[[Haibutsu kishaku]]", and the 1868 Temple Ordinance. Until that time, it was common in Japan for the same buildings to be used as both temples (Buddhist) and shrines (Shinto). Until the 19th century, Buddhist activities at the Togakushi Temple were dedicated to "[[:en:Avalokiteśvara|Avalokiteśvara]]".
== Site == Upon arrival at Togakushi it is recommended to first visit Oku-sha and then Kuzuryu-sha. It is a 2 kilometer hike from the entrance to the two shrines, however the path leading deep into the mountain can only be taken on foot. Beyond the cedar-lined path, you will be able to see the ''torii'' gate for Oku-sha at the bottom of the mountain and the ''shaden'' main building of the shrine as well. During winter the paths are closed but snowshoeing may be possible.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://naganonow.com/explore/togakushi-village-shrine/|title = Togakushi Village | Togakushi Shrine | Togakushi Ninja |Togakushi Ski Field|date = 17 December 2019}}</ref>
== See also == * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] * {{ill|Iigaoka Hachimangu|ja|飯香岡八幡宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iigaoka Hachimangu|qq=}} (the other [[Sōja shrine]] of [[:en:Kazusa Province|Kazusa Province]]) * [[Tamasaki Shrine]] ([[:en:Kazusa Province|Kazusa Province]] [[Ichinomiya]])
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Togakushi Shrine}} * [http://www.togakushi-jinja.jp/ 戸隠神社 (''Togakushi-jinja'') website] * [http://www.genbu.net/data/sinano/togakusi4_title.htm Togakushi-shrine's Pilgrimage stamp. gensyoushinokioku] in Japanese * [https://naganonow.com/explore/togakushi-village-shrine/ Togakushi Village | Togakushi Shrine] in English

Togakushi Temple was changed to a shrine by the Meiji government's Buddhism/Shinto separation initiatives "[[Shinbutsu bunri]]", "[[Haibutsu kishaku]]", and the 1868 Temple Ordinance. Until that time, it was common in Japan for the same buildings to be used as both temples (Buddhist) and shrines (Shinto). Until the 19th century, Buddhist activities at the Togakushi Temple were dedicated to "[[:en:Avalokiteśvara|Avalokiteśvara]]".
== Site == Upon arrival at Togakushi it is recommended to first visit Oku-sha and then Kuzuryu-sha. It is a 2 kilometer hike from the entrance to the two shrines, however the path leading deep into the mountain can only be taken on foot. Beyond the cedar-lined path, you will be able to see the ''torii'' gate for Oku-sha at the bottom of the mountain and the ''shaden'' main building of the shrine as well. During winter the paths are closed but snowshoeing may be possible.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://naganonow.com/explore/togakushi-village-shrine/|title = Togakushi Village | Togakushi Shrine | Togakushi Ninja |Togakushi Ski Field|date = 17 December 2019}}</ref>

Japan}} {{more citations needed|date=June 2022}} {{cleanup rewrite|date=May 2013}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{Nihongo|'''Togakushi Shrine'''|戸隠神社|Togakushi Jinja}} | image = File:160430 architecture_style = | festival = | prefecture = {{ill|Nagano|WD=Q128849}} | leadership = }} [[File:160430 Togakushi-jinja Chusha Nagano Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|The middle shrine Chū-sha]] [[File:Togakusi okusya.jpg|thumb|The upper shrine Oku-sha]] [[File:Tree lined path to the Togakushi shrine1.jpg|thumb|220x220px|Planted in the 17th century, the cedar-lined approach to the upper shrine Oku-sha]] The {{Nihongo|'''Togakushi Shrine'''|戸隠神社|Togakushi Jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Togakushi, Nagano|Togakushi]], [[:en:Nagano (city)|Nagano City]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The shrine is at the base of [[:en:Mount Togakushi|Mount Togakushi]] ({{Convert|1904|m|ft}}) in [[Myōkō-Togakushi Renzan National Park]].<ref>{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru Daijisen | title = Togakushiyama | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-12-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | oclc = 56431036 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }}</ref>
== Shrines == Togakushi Shrine consists of five shrines that can be visited in any order:
* The Lower Shrine (宝光社, ''Hōkō-sha'') enshrines the deity Ame-no-Uwaharu-no-Mikoto. It is believed to offer blessings related to academic life, [[:en:sewing|sewing]], safe [[:en:Childbirth|delivery]], and the protection of women and children.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=戸隠神社 {{!}} 霊山・戸隠山の麓を中心に創建された二千年余りに及ぶ歴史を刻む神社 |url=https://www.togakushi-jinja.jp/english/ |access-date=2024-08-20 |website=www.togakushi-jinja.jp |language=ja}}</ref> * ''Hinomiko-sha'' (火之御子社) enshrines four deities with the main deity being [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]]. There are two 500 year old [[:en:Cryptomeria|Japanese cedars]] known as the "husband and wife cedars" (夫婦の杉, ''Fufu-no-Sugi)'' and a famous [[:en:Cherry blossom|Cherry blossom]] tree known as [[:en:Saigyō|Saigyo]] Zakura. The shrine is believed to offer blessings for marriage, love, fire prevention, and entertainment.<ref name=":0" /> * The Middle Shrine (中社, ''Chū-sha'') has the head office of Togakushi Shrine. It enshrines [[Omoikane|Ame-no-Yagokoro-Omoikane-no-Mikoto]] and has several Japanese cedar trees ranging from 700 to 800 years old. It offers blessings for things such as academic achievement and business prosperity.<ref name=":0" /> * The Upper Shrine (奥社, ''Oku-sha'') enshrines Ame-no-Tachikarao-no-Mikoto. The approach to the shrine is about 2 kilometers long.<ref name=":1" /> The path to the shrine is lined with 400 year old Japanese cedars which are designated as [[Monuments of Japan|Natural Monuments of Japan]].<ref name=":1" /> About halfway through the approach to the shrine, there is a red gate called ''Zuishinmon'' (随神門). The shrine is believed to offer good fortune, good harvests, and victory in sports.<ref name=":1" /> * ''Kuzuryu-sha'' (九頭龍社) literally means "nine-headed dragon shrine." It is dedicated to [[:en:Kuzuryū|Kuzuryu-no-Okami]], the deity of water, rain, and the prevention of [[:en:tooth decay|tooth decay]].<ref name=":1" />
There is a ''[[Shuin|goshuin]]'' stamp available at each of the five shrines.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=戸隠神社 {{!}} 霊山・戸隠山の麓を中心に創建された二千年余りに及ぶ歴史を刻む神社 |url=https://www.togakushi-jinja.jp/ |access-date=2024-08-14 |website=www.togakushi-jinja.jp |language=ja}}</ref> Many of the shrines are separated by several kilometers, so personal vehicles and regularly-scheduled buses can be used for transportation between shrines.<ref>{{Cite web |title=アクセス |url=https://www.togakushi-jinja.jp/access/ |access-date=2024-08-20 |website=www.togakushi-jinja.jp |language=ja}}</ref>
It is the [[Sōja shrine]] of [[:en:Kazusa Province|Kazusa Province]].
==History== In one theory, the upper shrine, or ''Oku-sha'', is said to have been first constructed in the 5th year of the Emperor Kogen (210 BC) while Buddhist tradition holds that a monk named Gakumon discovered the Oku-sha area and began the practice of [[Shugendō|Shugendo]] there in the 2nd year of the Kasha era (849 AD). According to the [[:en:Nihon-Shoki|Nihon-Shoki]], the [[:en:Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]] had a map of the area made in 684 AD and a temporary building built the following year.
Togakushi shrine was a pilgrimage site during the following eight centuries. Its name was Ranked with the [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise-jingu]] Shrine, [[:en:Mount Kōya|Koya-san]] Temple and [[Enryaku-ji]] temples. Togakushisan Kansyuin Kenkou-ji was the formal name of the Togakushi Temple.
Two major esoteric Buddhist sects, [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]] and [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] fought for the hegemony of Togakushi Temple. Eventually the Shingon sect lost the battle. Togakushi Temple was changed to a shrine by the Meiji government's Buddhism/Shinto separation initiatives "[[Shinbutsu bunri]]", "[[Haibutsu kishaku]]", and the 1868 Temple Ordinance. Until that time, it was common in Japan for the same buildings to be used as both temples (Buddhist) and shrines (Shinto). Until the 19th century, Buddhist activities at the Togakushi Temple were dedicated to "[[:en:Avalokiteśvara|Avalokiteśvara]]".
== Site == Upon arrival at Togakushi it is recommended to first visit Oku-sha and then Kuzuryu-sha. It is a 2 kilometer hike from the entrance to the two shrines, however the path leading deep into the mountain can only be taken on foot. Beyond the cedar-lined path, you will be able to see the ''torii'' gate for Oku-sha at the bottom of the mountain and the ''shaden'' main building of the shrine as well. During winter the paths are closed but snowshoeing may be possible.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://naganonow.com/explore/togakushi-village-shrine/|title = Togakushi Village | Togakushi Shrine | Togakushi Ninja |Togakushi Ski Field|date = 17 December 2019}}</ref>

{{Nihongo|'''Togakushi Shrine'''|戸隠神社|Togakushi Jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Togakushi, Nagano|Togakushi]], [[:en:Nagano (city)|Nagano City]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The shrine is at the base of [[:en:Mount Togakushi|Mount Togakushi]] ({{Convert|1904|m|ft}}) in [[Myōkō-Togakushi Renzan National Park]].<ref>{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru Daijisen | title = Togakushiyama | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-12-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | oclc = 56431036 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }}</ref>
== Shrines == Togakushi Shrine consists of five shrines that can be visited in any order:
* The Lower Shrine (宝光社, ''Hōkō-sha'') enshrines the deity Ame-no-Uwaharu-no-Mikoto. It is believed to offer blessings related to academic life, [[:en:sewing|sewing]], safe [[:en:Childbirth|delivery]], and the protection of women and children.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=戸隠神社 {{!}} 霊山・戸隠山の麓を中心に創建された二千年余りに及ぶ歴史を刻む神社 |url=https://www.togakushi-jinja.jp/english/ |access-date=2024-08-20 |website=www.togakushi-jinja.jp |language=ja}}</ref> * ''Hinomiko-sha'' (火之御子社) enshrines four deities with the main deity being [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]]. There are two 500 year old [[:en:Cryptomeria|Japanese cedars]] known as the "husband and wife cedars" (夫婦の杉, ''Fufu-no-Sugi)'' and a famous [[:en:Cherry blossom|Cherry blossom]] tree known as [[:en:Saigyō|Saigyo]] Zakura. The shrine is believed to offer blessings for marriage, love, fire prevention, and entertainment.<ref name=":0" /> * The Middle Shrine (中社, ''Chū-sha'') has the head office of Togakushi Shrine. It enshrines [[Omoikane|Ame-no-Yagokoro-Omoikane-no-Mikoto]] and has several Japanese cedar trees ranging from 700 to 800 years old. It offers blessings for things such as academic achievement and business prosperity.<ref name=":0" /> * The Upper Shrine (奥社, ''Oku-sha'') enshrines Ame-no-Tachikarao-no-Mikoto. The approach to the shrine is about 2 kilometers long.<ref name=":1" /> The path to the shrine is lined with 400 year old Japanese cedars which are designated as [[Monuments of Japan|Natural Monuments of Japan]].<ref name=":1" /> About halfway through the approach to the shrine, there is a red gate called ''Zuishinmon'' (随神門). The shrine is believed to offer good fortune, good harvests, and victory in sports.<ref name=":1" /> * ''Kuzuryu-sha'' (九頭龍社) literally means "nine-headed dragon shrine." It is dedicated to [[:en:Kuzuryū|Kuzuryu-no-Okami]], the deity of water, rain, and the prevention of [[:en:tooth decay|tooth decay]].<ref name=":1" />
There is a ''[[Shuin|goshuin]]'' stamp available at each of the five shrines.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=戸隠神社 {{!}} 霊山・戸隠山の麓を中心に創建された二千年余りに及ぶ歴史を刻む神社 |url=https://www.togakushi-jinja.jp/ |access-date=2024-08-14 |website=www.togakushi-jinja.jp |language=ja}}</ref> Many of the shrines are separated by several kilometers, so personal vehicles and regularly-scheduled buses can be used for transportation between shrines.<ref>{{Cite web |title=アクセス |url=https://www.togakushi-jinja.jp/access/ |access-date=2024-08-20 |website=www.togakushi-jinja.jp |language=ja}}</ref>
It is the [[Sōja shrine]] of [[:en:Kazusa Province|Kazusa Province]].
==History== In one theory, the upper shrine, or ''Oku-sha'', is said to have been first constructed in the 5th year of the Emperor Kogen (210 BC) while Buddhist tradition holds that a monk named Gakumon discovered the Oku-sha area and began the practice of [[Shugendō|Shugendo]] there in the 2nd year of the Kasha era (849 AD). According to the [[:en:Nihon-Shoki|Nihon-Shoki]], the [[:en:Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]] had a map of the area made in 684 AD and a temporary building built the following year.
Togakushi shrine was a pilgrimage site during the following eight centuries. Its name was Ranked with the [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise-jingu]] Shrine, [[:en:Mount Kōya|Koya-san]] Temple and [[Enryaku-ji]] temples. Togakushisan Kansyuin Kenkou-ji was the formal name of the Togakushi Temple.
Two major esoteric Buddhist sects, [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]] and [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] fought for the hegemony of Togakushi Temple. Eventually the Shingon sect lost the battle. Togakushi Temple was changed to a shrine by the Meiji government's Buddhism/Shinto separation initiatives "[[Shinbutsu bunri]]", "[[Haibutsu kishaku]]", and the 1868 Temple Ordinance. Until that time, it was common in Japan for the same buildings to be used as both temples (Buddhist) and shrines (Shinto). Until the 19th century, Buddhist activities at the Togakushi Temple were dedicated to "[[:en:Avalokiteśvara|Avalokiteśvara]]".
== Site == Upon arrival at Togakushi it is recommended to first visit Oku-sha and then Kuzuryu-sha. It is a 2 kilometer hike from the entrance to the two shrines, however the path leading deep into the mountain can only be taken on foot. Beyond the cedar-lined path, you will be able to see the ''torii'' gate for Oku-sha at the bottom of the mountain and the ''shaden'' main building of the shrine as well. During winter the paths are closed but snowshoeing may be possible.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://naganonow.com/explore/togakushi-village-shrine/|title = Togakushi Village | Togakushi Shrine | Togakushi Ninja |Togakushi Ski Field|date = 17 December 2019}}</ref>

Togakushi Temple was changed to a shrine by the Meiji government's Buddhism/Shinto separation initiatives "[[Shinbutsu bunri]]", "[[Haibutsu kishaku]]", and the 1868 Temple Ordinance. Until that time, it was common in Japan for the same buildings to be used as both temples (Buddhist) and shrines (Shinto). Until the 19th century, Buddhist activities at the Togakushi Temple were dedicated to "[[:en:Avalokiteśvara|Avalokiteśvara]]".
== Site == Upon arrival at Togakushi it is recommended to first visit Oku-sha and then Kuzuryu-sha. It is a 2 kilometer hike from the entrance to the two shrines, however the path leading deep into the mountain can only be taken on foot. Beyond the cedar-lined path, you will be able to see the ''torii'' gate for Oku-sha at the bottom of the mountain and the ''shaden'' main building of the shrine as well. During winter the paths are closed but snowshoeing may be possible.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://naganonow.com/explore/togakushi-village-shrine/|title = Togakushi Village | Togakushi Shrine | Togakushi Ninja |Togakushi Ski Field|date = 17 December 2019}}</ref>

Nagano Prefecture, Japan}} {{more citations needed|date=June 2022}} {{cleanup rewrite|date=May 2013}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{Nihongo|'''Togakushi Shrine'''|戸隠神社|Togakushi Jinja}} |
